other_observers: Luke Pheneger, Will Anderson, Peter Gent, Peter Burke, Richard Trinkner, Bill Schmoker, and dozens of others
specific_location: Lagerman Reservoir and the Swede Lakes
description: First observed around 9:15am or so in the far southwest corner, in the rain and relatively dark conditions due to low clouds. At first I thought they were Long-billed Dowitchers because they were hunched over and probe-feeding dowitcher-style in a clustered group. But I noted they seemed a bit tall and lanky even when hunched over. They then flushed briefly and flew a short lap before resettling where they were, and while in flight they called, and I noted that it wasn't even close to LBDO calls - more like kek-keks instead of high-pitched chittering. I then figured they were Marbled Godwits, especially when I noted their slightly decurved bills, which still were not easy to discern in the suboptimal light at that distance. I did start to entertain thoughts of HUGO when I saw what seemed like a rich rufous belly coloration and relatively light, striped head on one of the birds, and then another, but I was still reluctant to call them that in these sketchy conditions. Finally, the birds flushed again and this time I followed their flight in the scope. They circled around and I could see the rufous bellies of some individuals more clearly, as well as wing striping. They landed on a somewhat nearer spit as a group, and in doing so all their tails flared out clearly showing the dark terminal band and white tail base, totally unlike Marbled Godwit. It wasn't a case of trying to single out one HUGO, they were ALL HUGOs. I was floored, and then had to arrive at a precise count. I then notified the Boulder birding community ASAP and prepared for the birder deluge. My tallies for other species then suffered as I remained totally fixated on these exquisite and hyper-rare visitors.
photographer: Photographer: Eric DeFonso, Eric DeFonso, Eric DeFonso, Eric DeFonso, Eric DeFonso
